local_names.romanized_local: Hā Duō Shì (Mandarin) / Haa1 Do1 Si2 (Cantonese)

region_of_origin: Hong Kong / Cantonese Chinese communities; widely served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Deep-fried dim sum — white bread triangles coated with seasoned shrimp paste and sesame seeds, deep-fried until golden

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for prawn toast. No exact or close-parent URI could be confirmed without fabrication. Closest conceptual parent would be a deep-fried bread-based seafood appetiser, but no confirmed FoodOn URI exists for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for the shrimp-paste component; the bread-frying composite is not individually coded in FoodEx2. Match type is closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for deep-fried shrimp-paste bread. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept confirmed for prawn toast as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of prawn toast is 4 triangles, weighing approximately 80 grams, as commonly served at Cantonese dim sum restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Toast (deep-fried) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for deep-fried prawn toast. This is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Micronutrients for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from enriched_json cross-reference at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White bread GI reference; analogous estimate applied to deep-fried white bread base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn toast as a composite dish. White bread GI is established at 71–75 (high) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021). Deep-frying gelatinises the bread surface and increases oil absorption, which does not substantially lower the GI of the white bread base. The shrimp paste protein and sesame seed fat provide a minor moderating effect. GI estimated at 75 (high). GL calculated as (75 × 27g available carbohydrate per 80g serving) / 100 = 20.25, rounded to 20.4 per input source. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 500 mg per 100g (400 mg per 80g serving) from shrimp paste and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Prawn toast contributes approximately 400 mg sodium per 4-triangle serving; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 triangles and avoid additional soy or sweet dipping sauces, which can add a further 200–400 mg sodium per tablespoon.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and high glycemic load (~20.4 per serving) from deep-fried white bread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn toast is among the least suitable dim sum choices for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high-GI white bread base, significant oil absorption during deep-frying (350 kcal/100g), and a glycemic load of ~20.4 per serving; steamed alternatives such as har gau or siu mai are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying oil absorption elevates total fat (18g/100g) and trans fat (0.1g/100g); saturated fat 3g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process causes significant oil absorption into the white bread sponge, raising total fat to 18g/100g with 3g saturated fat and 0.1g trans fat per 100g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit prawn toast to occasional consumption and choose steamed dim sum items as the primary option.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Elevated omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acids from deep-frying oil without corresponding omega-3 balance
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying in vegetable oils (corn/soybean) introduces omega-6 fatty acids that may promote pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production; the shrimp component provides selenium (12 mcg/100g) as an antioxidant benefit, but prawn toast is not recommended as a regular choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn toast (蝦多士 / 虾多士, 'Ha Do Si') is a classic Cantonese dim sum item and a staple of Westernized Chinese cuisine, widely served in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Chinese-British communities. It consists of small triangles of white bread topped with a savory paste made from minced shrimp (prawns) blended with water chestnuts, spring onions, and egg white for binding. The shrimp side is coated in raw sesame seeds before the entire piece is deep-fried in oil until golden and crisp. The result is a highly crunchy, fragrant, and savory appetizer with a rich contrast between the toasted sesame crust, the oil-saturated fried bread, and the succulent shrimp topping. At 350 kcal per 100g and 18g total fat per 100g, prawn toast is one of the most calorie-dense dim sum items per gram — the white bread acts as a sponge during deep-frying, absorbing a significant fraction of the cooking oil. Compared to steamed har gau (approximately 142 kcal/100g), prawn toast delivers 2.5 times more calories from the same gram weight, almost entirely due to oil absorption. The white bread base produces a high glycemic index (~75) and a high glycemic load (~20.4 per 4-triangle serving of 80g), making it one of the least suitable dim sum choices for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium is 500 mg per 100g (400 mg per serving), primarily from shrimp paste and seasoning. The shrimp component contributes selenium (12 mcg/100g) and protein (13g/100g). Not recommended for T2DM or cardiovascular disease patients as a regular item; steamed dim sum alternatives are strongly preferred.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference (GI 75, high), which is the approved GI source. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from enriched_json at confidence 0.9. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a deep-fried shrimp-paste white bread product.
notes: 1. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI (75, high) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for prawn toast as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 2. Copper (0.15 mg/100g), manganese (0.25 mg/100g), and vitamin K (2.5 mcg/100g) are sourced from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 rather than the primary HPB source. 3. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported by the primary source; set to -1. 4. Water content not reported by the primary source; set to -1. 5. Halal status is not confirmed — shrimp paste preparation and frying oil source may vary by establishment; is_halal set to false pending confirmation. 6. The foodo.sg URL is the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database portal; the specific entry for prawn toast (deep-fried) was accessed on 2026-04-13. 7. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally expected for a deep-fried bread-and-seafood composite product.
